Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

The entropy of this file measures how much information each symbol carries
on average. A file with uniform character distribution has high entropy,
while a file with many repeated characters has low entropy. Huffman coding
approaches the theoretical entropy limit.

Pufferfish is a command-line file compression utility powered by Huffman Coding.
It was built as a practical demonstration of several key concepts from
Discrete Mathematics and Information Theory.

This sample file can be used to test the compression and analysis features:

  pufferfish compress samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish analyze samples/sample.txt
  pufferfish extract samples/sample.puff

The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains
every letter of the English alphabet at least once, making it a useful test
for frequency analysis. Notice how common letters like 'e', 't', and 'o'
appear more frequently than rare letters like 'z', 'x', and 'q'.

In Huffman coding, more frequent symbols receive shorter binary codes,
while less frequent symbols receive longer codes. This is the core idea
behind the compression: we use fewer bits for common characters and more
bits for rare ones, resulting in a shorter overall encoding.

Here is some repeated text to demonstrate how Huffman coding handles
common patterns and achieves better compression ratios when certain
characters or sequences appear frequently in the input data.

ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
0123456789
